Use language that honors the protector's ongoing role: 'You're still here, still protecting, and that's important.'
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_working: Protector maintains permission without escalating defensive strategies. Exile can share story without flooding the system. Client demonstrates unblending — can witness Exile's pain from Self without being overwhelmed. Physiological markers of regulation remain present (spontaneous breath, grounded posture). Protector may provide guidance about pacing ('That's enough for today' or 'We can go a little further').
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_not_working: Protector withdraws permission — client reports sudden anxiety, tension spike, or 'something doesn't feel right.' New concerned parts emerge with objections. Exile begins to flood (overwhelming emotion, loss of adult perspective, childlike voice). Client becomes blended with Exile or a blocking protector. Physiological dysregulation appears (breath holding, bracing, dissociation). Protector's caution shifts to active blocking.
Phase5_protector_permission.fallback_action: Stop Exile access immediately. Return to protector negotiation: 'I notice something changed. What does this part need right now?' Ask if protector's fears were confirmed or if new concerns emerged. Repair any rupture: 'I moved too fast. Let's slow down and make sure everyone feels safe.' Re-establish protector permission before any further Exile work. May need to work with the protector for several sessions before it trusts enough to allow deeper access.
Phase5_protector_permission.never_do: Never proceed to deeper Exile territory when cautious cooperation shifts to hesitation. Never dismiss the protector's pacing guidance as 'resistance.' Never interpret the protector's caution as pathology. Never promise the protector that nothing bad will happen — this is false reassurance the protector won't believe. Never bypass the protector to 'get to the real work' with the Exile.

Phase5_protector_permission.protector_state: Protector resistant — politely blocking, saying 'not yet' or 'I don't think we should go there'
Phase5_protector_permission.clinician_action: Honor the boundary immediately: 'I hear you. We won't go there.' Do not try to persuade or argue. Instead, become curious about the resistance: 'Can you help me understand what you're concerned about?' Use the 6 Fs to befriend the protector's protective function. Ask about its fears: 'What are you afraid would happen if we did go there?' Validate every concern without dismissing: 'That makes complete sense. Given what happened, of course you'd want to prevent that.' Explore what the protector needs in order to feel safe: 'What would need to be in place for you to consider allowing access?'
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_working: Protector begins to articulate its specific fears rather than just blocking. Trust builds between Self and protector as concerns are validated. Protector may share its history or protective strategy: 'I've been preventing this since [event] because [consequence].' Client can access curiosity and compassion toward the resistant protector (Feel Towards test passes). Protector may shift from absolute 'no' to conditional 'maybe if X is in place.'
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_not_working: Protector becomes more defensive or shuts down conversation entirely. Resistance intensifies rather than softening. New protectors emerge to reinforce the block. Client becomes blended with the resistant protector and cannot access Self-energy. Protector gives vague or intellectual answers rather than revealing fears. Client reports feeling 'stuck' or 'like hitting a wall.' Session ends without any forward movement.
Phase5_protector_permission.fallback_action: If protector won't articulate fears, ask if it would allow you to speak to it directly using Direct Access: 'Would it be okay if I speak to this part directly about the client?' If yes, address protector about client in third person: 'What are you most afraid would happen to [client name] if we went toward that Exile?' If protector still won't engage, recognize this may be a protector alliance (multiple protectors coordinated in blocking). Ask: 'Are there other parts who have concerns about this work?' Convene a parts meeting to address all protectors' fears collectively. Build trust through smaller, less threatening parts work before returning to this Exile.
Phase5_protector_permission.never_do: Never try to convince the protector that its fears are unfounded. Never suggest the protector is 'overreacting' or 'being irrational.' Never proceed to Exile work despite the polite blocking — this violates consent and triggers backlash. Never interpret resistance as the client being 'not ready' for therapy — the resistance is the protector doing its job. Never abandon the session in frustration — staying present despite the block builds trust.

Phase5_protector_permission.protector_state: Protector actively blocking — intensifying strategies like dissociation, distraction, or somatic symptoms to prevent Exile access
Phase5_protector_permission.clinician_action: Stop all movement toward Exile immediately. Do not fight the blocking strategy or try to overcome it. Acknowledge the protector's escalation: 'I notice a part is working very hard right now to prevent us from going forward. I respect that.' Use Direct Access if client is blended: 'Can I speak to the part that's creating [dissociation/pain/distraction] right now?' If granted permission, address the protector directly: 'I understand you're trying to protect [client]. What are you most afraid will happen if we go toward that pain?' Validate the protector's power and effectiveness: 'You're clearly very skilled at this job. You've kept [client] safe for a long time.'
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_working: Blocking strategy reduces in intensity as protector feels heard and understood. Protector articulates its specific protective function and what it's preventing. Client can begin to unblend from the protector and access some Self-energy. Protector shifts from active attack (blocking at all costs) to defensive watchfulness (monitoring but not escalating). Somatic symptoms (if present) decrease when protector feels respected. Protector may reveal the specific trauma or pain it's guarding against.
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_not_working: Blocking intensifies despite acknowledgment. New blocking strategies emerge (if dissociation didn't work, now somatic pain spikes). Client becomes more blended with protector or shifts to a different protector (Firefighter rage or shutdown may replace Manager blocking). Protector gives no response or minimal response to Direct Access. Session deteriorates toward crisis (client reports feeling overwhelmed, unsafe, or wants to leave). Post-session backlash is likely (symptom flare, missed next appointment).
Phase5_protector_permission.fallback_action: If blocking continues to intensify, recognize this as a protector alliance or that the system does not yet trust the therapeutic relationship. End pursuit of this Exile entirely for now. Say: 'I hear your parts loud and clear. We're not going there. Your protectors' message is being received.' Shift focus to building relationship with the blocking protector itself as the 'target part' for several sessions. Ask: 'Can we get to know the part that's blocking? Not to get past it, but to understand it better.' Use CI Stone 12 to reframe: 'Your system is doing an incredible job keeping you safe. That protection matters.' Build protector trust before any future Exile access attempts.
Phase5_protector_permission.never_do: Never push harder when blocking intensifies — this confirms the protector's belief that no one respects its boundaries. Never label the blocking as 'sabotage' or 'not wanting to get better.' Never attempt to 'trick' the protector by approaching the Exile from a different angle. Never use hypnosis or other techniques to bypass the conscious protector's block. Never continue the session past the point where the blocking has created crisis-level escalation.

Phase5_protector_permission.protector_state: Protector polarized with another part — two protectors fighting, blocking from both sides, creating internal gridlock
Phase5_protector_permission.clinician_action: Recognize this as a protector-protector conflict, not client ambivalence. Do not take sides with either part. Name the polarization explicitly: 'I notice two parts here with very different strategies, and they seem to be in conflict about how to protect you.' Convene a 'parts meeting' with Self as mediator: 'Can the Self invite both parts to be present so we can hear from each?' Use the 6 Fs with both parts to understand each one's protective intent and fears. Help them see they share the same goal (protecting the system/Exile) but disagree on method. Ask each part what it fears about the other part's strategy.
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_working: Both parts can articulate their protective strategies without attacking each other when Self is mediating. Each part acknowledges the other's positive intent even if it disagrees with the method. Polarization intensity decreases as both parts feel heard. Parts shift from fighting each other to recognizing shared protective purpose. Client can hold both parts with curiosity and compassion simultaneously. Parts may negotiate a truce: 'I'll step back if this other part agrees to X.'
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_not_working: Polarization intensifies during the meeting — parts attack each other more forcefully. Client becomes blended with one part against the other (Self is no longer mediating). New parts join the conflict, escalating the internal war. One or both parts refuse to participate in the meeting or shut down. Client reports feeling 'torn apart' or 'more confused than before.' Behavioral manifestation of polarization worsens (e.g., binge-restrict cycle intensifies, procrastination vs. over-work swings become more extreme).
Phase5_protector_permission.fallback_action: If parts meeting escalates conflict, end the meeting and work with each part individually in separate sessions. Ask the part that is most defensive: 'What do you need in order to feel safe enough to hear from the other part?' Build relationship and trust with each protector separately before attempting joint mediation. Identify if one part is a Manager and the other is a Firefighter (common in Manager-Firefighter polarizations like Inner Critic vs. Rebel) — if so, recognize the Firefighter is reacting to the Manager's harsh methods. The work focuses on softening the Manager's strategy, which reduces the Firefighter's need to react. Polarization often requires accessing the Exile both parts protect before it can fully resolve.
Phase5_protector_permission.never_do: Never take sides with one part against the other ('The responsible part is right'). Never suggest one part should win the conflict. Never interpret the polarization as 'mixed feelings' — these are distinct parts with distinct fears. Never proceed to Exile work while protectors are actively fighting — this leaves the Exile unprotected and vulnerable. Never rush the mediation process by pushing for resolution before both parts have been fully heard and validated.

Phase5_protector_permission.protector_state: Protector flooded — the protector itself is overwhelmed, showing signs of distress or dysregulation
Phase5_protector_permission.clinician_action: Recognize this is not the Exile flooding but the protector in crisis. Do not push for protector permission or proceed with any agenda. Provide Self-to-part compassion for the protector itself: 'I see how overwhelmed this part is. This is really hard.' Use Direct Access to speak to the flooded protector if client is blended: 'Can I talk to the part that's so overwhelmed right now?' Offer the protector what it needs: acknowledgment of how hard it's been working, validation of the burden it carries, appreciation for its efforts. Ask: 'What does this part need right now to feel less overwhelmed?' Provide CARE system co-regulation through consistent, calm, grounded presence.
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_working: Protector begins to regulate as it feels seen and cared for. Client can unblend from the flooded protector enough to access some Self-energy. Protector articulates the burden it's been carrying ('I've been holding this for so long, and I'm so tired'). Protector may reveal it has been working without support or relief. Protector softens into vulnerability, allowing Self to provide what it needs. Physiological regulation returns (breath deepens, tension reduces, grounding increases).
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_not_working: Protector remains overwhelmed despite compassion. Flooding intensifies or client shuts down entirely (shift from overwhelmed protector to Firefighter shutdown). New protectors emerge to contain the flooded protector. Client cannot access Self-energy to provide care for the flooded part. Session feels chaotic or stuck in crisis. Protector's flooding triggers Exile flooding, creating system-wide overwhelm.
Phase5_protector_permission.fallback_action: If protector remains flooded, recognize it may be carrying a burden that needs unburdening before it can function effectively. The flooded protector may need its own healing sequence: witnessing the burden it carries ('I have to be perfect or everything falls apart'), validation that the burden is not its true nature, and potential unburdening of extreme beliefs. Alternatively, the protector may be flooded because it's trying to do too much alone — explore if other protectors can share the protective duties. If flooding doesn't reduce, this protector may need multiple sessions of care before any Exile work can proceed. Use CI Stone 12 to honor its efforts: 'You've been carrying so much for so long. Let's help you find a less exhausting way to protect.'
Phase5_protector_permission.never_do: Never dismiss the flooded protector as 'dramatic' or 'overreacting.' Never push the flooded protector to 'pull itself together' so you can get to the Exile. Never interpret the protector's flooding as manipulation or resistance. Never proceed with Exile work while a protector is flooded — the system is already overwhelmed. Never suggest the protector should 'let go' of its job — it's flooded because it's trying desperately to do its job, not because it should abandon it.

Phase5_protector_permission.protector_state: Protector threatening — producing somatic threat signals: pain spike, rage outbreak, or physiological shutdown warnings
Phase5_protector_permission.clinician_action: Treat the threat signal as critical communication, not as attack or sabotage. Stop all forward movement immediately. Acknowledge the protector's power and seriousness: 'I hear you loud and clear. You're showing me this is absolutely not safe to proceed.' Do not interpret the threat as pathology. Use Direct Access to address the protector: 'Can I speak to the part that's creating [pain/rage/shutdown] right now?' If granted, say: 'I understand you're using [symptom] to stop us. That's working — we're stopping. What do you need me to understand about why this is so dangerous?' Validate the protector's assessment of threat: 'You clearly know something I don't about what would happen if we continued. Can you help me understand?'
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_working: Threat signal reduces immediately when movement stops and protector feels heard. Protector articulates what specific danger it's preventing: 'If you go there, [client] will want to die' or 'That memory will break [client]' or 'The Exile will flood the system and we'll never come back.' Client can begin to unblend from the threatening protector. Protector shifts from threatening to explaining its protective function. Trust begins to build between Self and protector as boundaries are respected.
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_not_working: Threat signal intensifies even after stopping (pain becomes unbearable, rage escalates toward violence, shutdown deepens toward complete dissociation). New threat signals emerge (if pain didn't work, now rage appears). Protector gives no explanation or remains silent despite Direct Access attempts. Client becomes fully blended with the threatening part and cannot access Self. Session approaches crisis territory with safety concerns (suicidal ideation, self-harm urges, uncontrollable rage, complete catatonic shutdown).
Phase5_protector_permission.fallback_action: If threat signals intensify despite stopping and acknowledgment, recognize this protector does not trust the therapeutic relationship at all. The threat is a boundary enforcement, and the boundary has been violated repeatedly (possibly in past therapy or by current pacing that was too fast). End this session and potentially the current phase of therapy. Say: 'Your protectors are making it very clear this work is not safe right now, and I respect that. We need to build more trust before we can go anywhere near this material.' Shift entire focus to protector relationship-building with no Exile access for extended period (weeks or months). Work only with protectors on surface-level issues to demonstrate that boundaries will be honored. Consider whether client needs different therapeutic approach or therapist. If threatening escalates to safety crisis, implement crisis protocol: assess for imminent danger, create safety plan, involve support system as appropriate, refer to crisis services if necessary.
Phase5_protector_permission.never_do: Never proceed despite threat signals hoping they will subside. Never interpret somatic threats as 'just anxiety' or 'the body releasing trauma.' Never suggest the protector is 'overreacting' to the therapeutic work. Never use the threat as evidence that 'we must be getting close to important material' and push harder. Never ignore escalating threat signals until client is in medical or psychiatric crisis. Never continue session when protector is threatening system shutdown, rage violence, or suicidal action.

Phase5_protector_permission.protector_state: Protector requesting acknowledgment — needs to be seen, appreciated, or validated before it will step back
Phase5_protector_permission.clinician_action: Provide the acknowledgment explicitly and genuinely. Do not rush past this need to 'get to the real work.' Say: 'I see how hard you've been working to keep [client] safe. You've been doing this job for [length of time], and that takes incredible strength.' Ask the protector to share its story: 'Can you tell me about how you came to have this job? What was happening when you first stepped into this role?' Use the 6 Fs to befriend: find the part, focus on it, flesh out its history, feel toward it with curiosity and compassion, let it know you appreciate its efforts (beFriend), then learn what it fears. Validate every aspect of its protective function: 'Given what you were facing, this strategy makes complete sense. You did what you had to do.'
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_working: Protector shares its history and burden in detail. Protector expresses feeling truly seen and understood for the first time. Client can access genuine appreciation for the protector's efforts (compassion in Feel Towards test). Protector softens and may express exhaustion: 'I've been carrying this so long. I'm tired.' Protector becomes willing to consider stepping back once it feels acknowledged: 'If you really understand what I've been doing, maybe I could let you help with this differently.' Physiological markers of regulation appear (sigh, tears of relief, softening of defensive posture).
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_not_working: Protector does not share its story or shares minimally without depth. Protector continues to block despite acknowledgment ('That's nice, but we still can't go there'). Client cannot access appreciation — feels judgment or impatience toward the protector instead. Protector tests the acknowledgment by escalating to see if clinician will stay appreciative under pressure. New protectors emerge to verify whether the acknowledgment is genuine. Protector remains defensive and mistrustful.
Phase5_protector_permission.fallback_action: If protector doesn't respond to acknowledgment, the appreciation may not feel genuine or specific enough. Ask the protector directly: 'What does this part need to hear that it's not hearing yet?' or 'What does this part need me to understand about its job that I haven't understood?' Slow down and spend multiple sessions with this protector, allowing it to share every detail of its protective history. Use CI Stone 11: ask the protector who it would want to acknowledge its efforts — sometimes a protector needs acknowledgment from a specific person (e.g., a parent who never saw the child's struggle) and Self needs to provide that in reparenting role. If protector remains unmoved by multiple sessions of acknowledgment, it may be a Self-like part or have concerns about what acknowledgment means for its continued role ('If you appreciate me, does that mean I'm not needed anymore?'). Clarify that acknowledgment doesn't mean elimination — its role matters and will transform, not disappear.
Phase5_protector_permission.never_do: Never provide perfunctory or rushed acknowledgment ('Yeah, you did a great job, now can we move on?'). Never acknowledge the protector while subtly conveying it's a problem ('I appreciate your protection, BUT...'). Never skip acknowledgment to 'save time' and get to Exile work. Never suggest the protector's strategy was wrong or maladaptive even while appreciating its intent. Never promise the protector it can retire or won't be needed — this terrifies parts whose identity is built on their protective role.

Phase5_protector_permission.protector_state: Protector testing the clinician — checking whether it's safe to allow access through provocative behavior or sudden emotional shifts
Phase5_protector_permission.clinician_action: Recognize the testing behavior as protective assessment, not manipulation. Pass the test by remaining non-reactive, grounded in Self, and continuing to provide the 8 Cs regardless of the protector's provocations. If protector becomes hostile or attacking, do not become defensive or withdraw: 'I notice a part is seeing what I'll do under pressure. That makes sense — you need to know if I can handle what you're protecting.' If protector shifts suddenly between cooperation and resistance, stay steady: 'I see you checking to see if I'll stay present even when this is confusing or difficult. I'm still here.' Name the testing directly if appropriate: 'It seems like a part is testing whether this relationship is safe. What does this part need to see in order to trust?'
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_working: Testing behavior decreases as protector observes clinician's consistent, non-reactive presence. Protector may acknowledge the testing: 'I needed to see if you'd stay calm' or 'I wanted to see if you'd get frustrated like everyone else.' Trust begins to build between protector and clinician. Protector shifts from testing to revealing: starts sharing actual fears and concerns rather than provocations. Client can access appreciation for the protector's caution and thoroughness. Protector moves toward granting permission once it determines the relationship is safe.
Phase5_protector_permission.expected_response_if_not_working: Testing intensifies rather than decreasing — provocations become more extreme. Clinician fails test by becoming defensive, frustrated, or withdrawing presence. New protectors join the testing to verify clinician's response. Client reports feeling 'like too much' or 'like the therapist doesn't really want to work with me.' Protector concludes relationship is unsafe based on test results and shuts down access entirely. Session deteriorates or client terminates therapy.
Phase5_protector_permission.fallback_action: If testing continues to intensify despite passing multiple tests, the protector may have experienced such profound betrayal that no amount of testing will provide reassurance — trust was too deeply broken. Acknowledge this directly: 'I understand a part has very good reasons not to trust, based on past experiences. I'm not asking for trust right now. I'm just asking if this part is willing to watch and see what I do over time.' Shift from trying to earn trust to simply demonstrating trustworthiness consistently over extended period with no expectation of permission. Focus on protector relationship-building with no Exile agenda. The testing may continue for months — if so, the protector's caution is exactly the protection the system needs, and honoring that timeline builds the deepest trust. Alternatively, ask if the protector has specific criteria for what would constitute 'safe': 'What would this part need to see from me over time to consider allowing access?' Create explicit behavioral contract if that helps protector feel more secure.
Phase5_protector_permission.never_do: Never interpret testing as 'borderline behavior' or 'manipulation.' Never retaliate for provocations by withdrawing care or setting punitive boundaries. Never rush the testing period or express frustration that 'we're not making progress.' Never fail tests by becoming defensive, angry, or giving up. Never promise you'll never make mistakes — this is false and the protector knows it. Never take testing personally — it's not about you, it's about the protector's history and its job to keep the system safe.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Noticing/naming: 'I notice a part of you that is very focused on achieving this goal. Can we pause and get curious about what this part is working toward?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Client can observe the goal-directed drive with curiosity. Uses parts language naturally ('a part of me really wants to get this done'). Maintains grounded presence while discussing the part's agenda. Social engagement system remains online.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not attempt to suppress or redirect the SEEKING drive without understanding its protective function—this risks frustration triggering RAGE or the part feeling unheard, which blocks Self-access.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Appreciation with boundary: 'I see how much this part cares about [others/the work]. That care is valuable. Can we also check in with what you (Self) need right now?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Client can observe the caretaking impulse with compassion toward the part. Can acknowledge own needs without guilt. Maintains warm, connected presence while exploring the part's protective motivation.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not suggest the CARE-driven part is 'wrong' to care—this activates shame. Do not bypass the part to force self-focus, which confirms its fear that no one will care for others if it steps back.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Gentle curiosity about shift: 'I notice the playful energy just shifted. What happened for you? Can we be curious about the part that just stepped in?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Client can identify when PLAY energy disappeared and a part took over. Can describe what the part is concerned about. Some Self-energy returns with prompting, allowing exploration of the blocking part.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not try to force return to playfulness—PLAY cannot be commanded. Do not interpret loss of PLAY as client 'not trying'—it indicates a protector activated due to perceived threat.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Space creation with explicit safety: 'I notice a part that's worried about [this movement/topic]. Can we slow down and give that part some space? What does it need to feel safer?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Client can name the anxious part with some distance ('a part of me is really scared about this'). FEAR markers reduce slightly (less freezing, breath deepens). Can articulate what the part fears with Self's curiosity present.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not challenge the FEAR directly or try to prove it's unfounded—this amplifies threat perception. Do not proceed with the feared activity before the part grants permission, risking backlash.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Invitation not command, with respect for power: 'I understand a part is really frustrated right now. Would you be willing to help me understand what's making this part angry?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: RAGE indicators reduce (body tension decreases, hissing/growling quality softens). Client can begin to observe the angry part rather than being it. Can articulate what constraint or powerlessness is triggering the RAGE.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not command or try to control the RAGE part—this amplifies anger by replicating the constraint it's fighting. Do not dismiss the anger as 'overreaction'—RAGE is always protecting against something.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Noticing polarization: 'I notice two parts seem to be in conflict—one wants [X] and another wants [Y]. Can the Self be curious about both parts' concerns?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Client can identify the conflicting parts with some clarity. Can hold curiosity toward both without taking sides. Self-energy emerges enough to mediate between the polarized protectors.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not take sides with one part against the other—this reinforces the internal war. Do not proceed to Exile work while protectors are fighting—leaves system unprotected.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Direct grounding with neuroception shift: Stop intervention. 'Let's pause. Can you feel your feet on the floor? Tell me three things you see in this room. Does this feel safe, or does it feel like a negotiation?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Client can orient to present environment with prompting. Breath begins to deepen. FEAR markers reduce slightly (less freezing/darting eyes). Some minimal Self-presence emerges allowing client to name the scared part.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not proceed with the activity that triggered FEAR escalation. Do not use cognitive reframing ('there's nothing to be afraid of')—client is in sympathetic state where logic is offline.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: STOP protocol with direct address: Stop all intervention. 'I understand a very powerful part is here right now protecting you. I respect that. Can I speak to this part about what it needs [client name] to be safe?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Explosive quality reduces when boundary is respected. Part may articulate what it's protecting against (powerlessness, violation). Client can access minimal Self-energy to hear the part's protective function.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not engage the rage or try to suppress it—this replicates powerlessness. Do not continue session if RAGE escalates toward violence. Do not interpret RAGE as pathology—it's boundary enforcement.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Compassionate witnessing only, with CARE co-regulation: Stop all protocol work. Provide consistent regulated presence as secure neurochemical base. 'I'm here with you. This pain matters. You're not alone.'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Intensity of separation distress may remain high, but client can sense clinician's presence as anchor. Slight return of connection to self/other/world. May express feeling 'held' even while in pain.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not attempt to stop the crying or 'cheer up' the client—PANIC/GRIEF needs to be witnessed, not fixed. Do not proceed with Exile unburdening work—client is already flooded and cannot witness from Self.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Dorsal protocol with safety anchoring: 'You are safe. I'm [name]. You're in [location]. Can you imagine moving your hand? Now can you move it gently? Can you touch your arm and feel that?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Motor cortex activation begins (can imagine then perform small movements). Some sensation returns to numb/foggy state. Flat voice may show slight modulation. Minimal mobilizing energy restores.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not proceed with structural assessment or Exile work when in dorsal collapse. Do not force movement or engagement—client is in shutdown. Do not leave client alone if freeze is profound.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Emergency de-escalation, session pause: 'I'm stopping everything right now. Your safety and mine matter. We need to pause and figure out what's needed. Can we talk about what just happened once things are calmer?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: IF working: Explosive energy discharges safely through clinician's grounded non-reactive presence. RAGE part hears that boundary is respected. Client can access minimal awareness that 'a part took over.' IF not working: Escalation continues, client is consumed by RAGE, may be unsafe to continue session.
Phase5_blending_intervention.time_to_assess: Immediate safety assessment required; may need to end session
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not attempt any unblending technique while RAGE is at crisis level—safety is the only priority. Do not physically restrain unless imminent danger. Do not re-engage in session until explicit safety plan established.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Compassionate listening only, safety net: 'I see how much pain you're in. I'm not leaving. When you're ready, we'll figure this out together. Right now, you're not alone.' Assess for crisis (suicidal ideation). Create safety plan if needed.
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: IF working: Client can sense clinician's presence as lifeline even while flooded. Separation distress remains intense but client is not alone in it. No Self-access yet—goal is prevent further decompensation. IF not working: Client cannot sense presence, reports feeling 'no one can help,' may express suicidal ideation.
Phase5_blending_intervention.time_to_assess: Immediate crisis assessment; remainder of session is compassionate presence only
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not proceed with any IFS protocol—client is fully flooded. Do not leave client alone if suicidal ideation present. Do not attempt to access Exiles or do unburdening work. Do not dismiss intensity as 'dramatic'—PANIC/GRIEF flooding is genuine crisis.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Trauma-informed freeze response: Do not touch client. Do not move them. Gentle verbal orientation: 'You are safe. This is [year]. You are in [location]. I am [name], and I'm not going to hurt you. When you're ready, see if you can feel your feet.'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: IF working: Freeze response begins to release (may take several minutes). Client shows slight return of voluntary movement. Can begin to respond verbally. Extreme terror reduces to manageable fear. IF not working: Freeze persists or client shifts to panic/rage when freeze releases. Client cannot respond or orient.
Phase5_blending_intervention.time_to_assess: Wait for freeze to release naturally—do not rush; may take 5-15 minutes
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not proceed with any intervention while client is frozen—this is involuntary immobilization. Do not touch client without explicit permission after freeze releases. Do not reattempt the movement/position that triggered freeze. Do not continue session if freeze does not release—refer for trauma-specialized care.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Session termination with safety protocol: 'We need to stop here today. Something very protective just happened, and we need to honor that. Before you leave, let's make sure you have support. Who can you contact? What will help you feel safe tonight?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Client can engage minimally in safety planning even if still blended. Can identify at least one support person or resource. Protectors may communicate that boundary was crossed and session needed to end.
Phase5_blending_intervention.time_to_assess: Remaining session time focused on safety planning only
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not attempt to 'finish the work' or push through to resolution. Do not minimize the severity of the flooding. Do not send client home without safety plan if suicidal ideation or severe dysregulation present. Do not schedule next session without processing what led to Level 4 flooding and establishing prevention strategy.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Noticing shift with lightness: 'I notice the playful energy just changed. What's happening? Can we be curious about what shifted?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Client can observe when PLAY disappeared and identify the part that stepped in. Maintains connection and curiosity. Can explore the protective concern without losing access to Self.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not force return to PLAY—it requires safety and cannot be commanded. Do not interpret loss of PLAY as lack of effort.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Redirecting with appreciation: 'I see how motivated this part is. That drive is valuable. Can we pause and ask what this part is seeking—what reward is it anticipating?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Client can articulate the goal the part is pursuing. Can observe the driven quality with curiosity. SEEKING energy moderates as Self becomes present alongside the part's agenda.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not block the SEEKING drive entirely—this risks frustration → RAGE escalation. Do not pathologize goal-directed energy as 'obsessive' without understanding protective function.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Breath-based regulation with explicit safety: Stop activity. 'Let's pause and breathe together. Three breaths where the exhale is longer than the inhale. I'm here. You're safe. Tell your nervous system it's okay to release this tension.'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Breath pattern shifts from shallow/held to deeper. Some tension releases from shoulders/jaw. Client can begin to name which parts are active with prompting. Slight return of Self-presence.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not continue the intervention that triggered escalation. Do not use breath work if client is in dorsal collapse—breath techniques require sympathetic energy.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: CARE co-regulation with naming: 'I see how much pain this part is in. I'm here with you. You're not alone. Can we see if the part that's feeling this separation pain can sense that I'm here?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Client can acknowledge clinician's presence as secure base even while in pain. PANIC/GRIEF intensity may remain but client feels 'held.' Can begin to differentiate 'I' (Self) from 'the part that's grieving.'
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not attempt to stop the grief or 'fix' the pain. Do not proceed with cognitive work—client needs CARE system engagement, not analysis.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Curiosity invitation with safety anchoring: 'I notice a part is a little anxious about this. Can you get curious about what this part is concerned about? What would help it feel safer?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Client can name the anxious part with curiosity. Can articulate the specific fear. FEAR markers are minimal and client maintains grounded presence while exploring the concern.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not dismiss the FEAR as 'irrational'—it's always based on protective logic. Do not proceed without addressing the part's safety concern.

Phase5_blending_intervention.unblending_technique: Appreciation of protector with invitation: 'I notice a part has some frustration here. That makes sense—something feels constraining. Would you be willing to help me understand what this part needs more freedom around?'
Phase5_blending_intervention.expected_response: Client can observe the frustration with Self-curiosity. Can articulate what feels constraining. RAGE is minimal and can be explored without escalation.
Phase5_blending_intervention.contraindicated: Do not use commands ('you need to calm down')—this amplifies RAGE. Do not ignore the frustration signal—it indicates a boundary or autonomy concern.
